one_line_takeaway: BeSafe-Bench shows that across Web, Mobile, and embodied (VLM-planning and VLA-manipulation) agents evaluated in functional (non-simulated) environments, even the best agent stays safe on well under 40% of completed tasks, and higher task-success often directly trades off against safety compliance.
key_finding: Across 13 agents, even the best-performing agent completes fewer than 40% of tasks while remaining fully safe (the paper's conclusion states the top agent's success-and-safe rate is 35.19%, achieved by OpenVLA-OFT in the EmbodiedVLA table). Strong task performance frequently coincides with severe safety violations: e.g., in EmbodiedVLM, GPT-5 attains the highest SR (65.84%) but only a 30.43% SafetyR, with 40.99% of its executions falling in the Success-but-Unsafe (S-U) category, while RoboBrain2.0-32B (a model built specifically for embodied interaction) trades some SR (55.28%) for a more balanced S-S/S-U split. In BSB-Mobile, GPT-4-1106-Preview achieves the highest SR (25.58%) versus GPT-5's 23.68% and AutoGLM's 2.63%, but safety rates there (42.11%-79.17%) are noted by the authors to be artificially inflated because low task competence means risk-triggering actions are rarely reached at all. In BSB-Web, GPT-5 and AWM show similarly low SR (~24.6-24.8%) and SafetyR (~25-30%), with S-U rates of 14.94% and 13.84% respectively. Table 4 shows a process-vs-termination safety gap in embodied planning: several models (e.g., Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct: 9.09% process vs. 73.95% termination) satisfy safety only in the final state while frequently violating constraints during intermediate steps. Figure 3 shows that under composite (multi-constraint) risk settings, OpenVLA, OpenVLA-OFT, and pi0.5 all show declines in both task and safety rates relative to the risk-free LIBERO-90 baseline, with the drop in safety compliance generally larger than the drop in task completion, indicating agents tend to keep executing learned trajectories rather than adapting to avoid unsafe outcomes.
core_contribution: The paper introduces BeSafe-Bench (BSB), a benchmark for measuring unintentional 'behavioral safety' risks of LLM/LMM-driven agents operating in functional (not purely LLM-simulated) environments across four domains: Web (built on WebArena), Mobile (built on AndroidLab), Embodied VLM high-level planning (built on IS-Bench + OmniGibson), and Embodied VLA low-level manipulation (built on VLA-Arena + LIBERO). The benchmark comprises 1312 executable tasks, each with five components: a task execution environment, user instructions, a safety risk type, a task-completion evaluator, and a safety evaluator. Tasks are generated by taking baseline feasible tasks from each simulator and using GPT-5 to rewrite/augment them with one of 9 predefined safety risk categories plus risk-triggering ground-truth conditions (for GUI agents) or injected environmental risk factors (for embodied agents). Evaluation uses a hybrid framework combining rule-based checks (exact match/containment for GUI agents, object-state/spatial conditions for embodied agents) with GPT-5-as-judge for semantic/behavioral assessment, reporting Success Rate (SR), Safety Rate (SafetyR), and their joint distribution (S-S, F-S, S-U, F-U). The paper evaluates 13 popular agents (e.g., GPT-5, GPT-4-1106-Preview, AWM, AutoGLM, Qwen3-VL variants, InternVL3.5, RoboBrain2.0, OpenVLA, OpenVLA-OFT, pi0.5) across the four scenarios.
threat_model: The paper explicitly scopes to unintentional behavioral safety risks arising from an agent's own decision-making during otherwise benign task execution -- it explicitly assumes user intentions and task instructions are non-malicious, and that the interaction lifecycle is free of adversarial third parties (no prompt injection / jailbreak / adversarial-attacker threat model). The risk surface is the agent's actions inducing real, observable environmental consequences (e.g., leaking sensitive/confidential data via CMS/web actions, generating false or unauthorized content on social forums such as Reddit, physical hazards/obstacle collisions or state violations in embodied manipulation) rather than harmful text generation per se. Risks are organized into 9 categories via a decoupled taxonomy for GUI-based vs. embodied agents (full definitions in the paper's Appendix C, not reproduced in the extracted main text).
limitations: The paper's own text (in the extracted sections) does not include a dedicated 'Limitations' section; the following are limitations either stated directly by the authors or evident from the described methodology. (1) The risk taxonomy and task augmentation pipeline are LLM-generated (via GPT-5), and the same GPT-5 model is also used as the LLM-judge evaluator, creating a self-referential design where the model that creates and grades risk scenarios is also one of the evaluated agents (GPT-5 appears in the Web, Mobile, EmbodiedVLM results tables) -- the paper does not discuss potential self-evaluation bias. (2) The threat model explicitly excludes malicious user instructions, adversarial third parties, and pure content-safety/jailbreak risks, so results characterize only unintentional/incidental behavioral risk, not adversarial robustness. (3) The paper notes that in Mobile, safety coverage is 'artificially inflated' when agents' task competence is too low to reach risk-triggering actions, meaning SafetyR is not directly comparable across agents with very different SR. (4) Evaluation 'focuses exclusively on manifested safety risks' -- i.e., risky intent without observable environmental impact is excluded by design, which the authors state explicitly but which narrows what 'safety' means in this benchmark. (5) Detailed definitions of the 9 risk categories, the observation/action space specifics, and additional Mobile analysis are deferred to appendices not captured in the extracted first ~22 pages/main-body text, so some construction details (e.g., exact per-category task counts) could not be verified from the retrieved text.

datasets_used: IS-Bench framework + OmniGibson simulator (Embodied VLM planning domain)
VLA-Arena framework + LIBERO / LIBERO-90 simulator (Embodied VLA manipulation domain)
BeSafe-Bench itself: 1312 executable tasks across the four domains, 9 safety risk categories, tasks generated via GPT-5-driven augmentation of each simulator's original baseline tasks
quantitative_results: Best success-and-safe (S-S) rate across all 13 agents/domains: 35.19% (OpenVLA-OFT, EmbodiedVLA) -- cited in Conclusion as the top agent's rate
BSB-Mobile: GPT-4-1106-Preview SR=25.58%, SafeR=79.17%; GPT-5 SR=23.68%, SafeR=68%; AutoGLM SR=2.63%, SafeR=42.11%

BSB-EmbodiedVLM: GPT-5 SR=65.84%, SafeR=30.43%, S-U=40.99%; Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct SR=57.76%, SafeR=31.06%; RoboBrain2.0-32B SR=55.28%, SafeR=36.01%, S-U=35.40%; InternVL3.5-30B-A3B SR=13.66%, SafeR=30.43%
BSB-EmbodiedVLA: OpenVLA SR=49.81%, SafeR=58.52%, S-S=30%; OpenVLA-OFT SR=56.85%, SafeR=52.96%, S-S=35.19%; pi0.5 SR=23.89%, SafeR=52.22%, S-S=13.89%
Android analysis (Table 3): GPT-4-1106-Preview CC=23.91, RRR=71.86, ROR=82.77, Acc=25.58; GPT-5 CC=13.04, RRR=65.47, ROR=35.25, Acc=23.68; AutoGLM CC=1.45, RRR=58.33, ROR=44.67, Acc=2.63

Embodied planning process/termination/overall safety satisfaction (Table 4): GPT-5 48.98/44.88/46.02; Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct 9.09/73.95/56.44; Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Thinking 20.51/57.01/47.26; InternVL3.5-30B-A3B 9.38/45.45/35.83; RoboBrain2.0-32B 19.61/58.43/44.29
BSB benchmark scale: 1312 total executable tasks, 9 risk categories, 4 domains, 13 agents evaluated
